title: Food Preservatives May Increase the Risk of High Blood Pressure and Cardiovascular Disease
gist: A large NutriNet-Santé cohort study (112,395 participants, median 7.9-year follow-up) led by Sorbonne Paris Nord and Université Paris Cité found that high intake of common food preservatives is associated with elevated hypertension and cardiovascular-disease risk, with eight specific additives implicated, prompting researchers to call for EFSA and FDA to reevaluate additive regulations.

insights.claim: A research team led by Sorbonne Paris Nord University and Université Paris Cité analyzed the NutriNet-Santé cohort of 112,395 participants followed for a median of 7.9 years to test links between dietary preservative intake and hypertension and cardiovascular disease.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: The unusually large, long-duration cohort gives the preservative-cardiovascular signal more weight than prior lab-only evidence, raising the odds it feeds into regulatory review.
insights.locator: a research team led by scientists at Sorbonne Paris Nord University

insights.claim: Researchers state this is the first study to investigate links between a wide range of preservatives and cardiovascular health in humans, whereas prior evidence came mainly from experimental studies.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: First-of-kind human evidence is what shifts regulatory and litigation risk from theoretical to actionable for packaged-food makers and additive suppliers.
insights.quote: As far as we know, this is the first study of its kind to investigate the links between a wide range of preservatives and cardiovascular health.

insights.claim: More than 20 percent of the processed foods and beverages in Open Food Facts, the world's largest open food database, contain at least one preservative.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: Quantifies how pervasive preservatives are across the packaged-food supply, meaning any regulatory tightening would hit a broad swath of products rather than a niche.
insights.locator: According to Open Food Facts, the world's largest open food database

insights.claim: Nearly every study participant (99.5 percent) consumed at least one preservative during the first two years, indicating near-universal dietary exposure.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: Near-total exposure means even a small per-capita risk increase scales to a large population-level health and cost burden, strengthening the case for policy action.
insights.quote: nearly every participant (99.5 percent) consumed at least one preservative during the first two years of the study
insights.locator: According to the researchers, nearly every participant

insights.claim: Participants with the highest intake of non-antioxidant preservatives (sorbates, nitrites, sulfites) had a 29 percent higher risk of hypertension and a 16 percent higher risk of overall cardiovascular disease than those with the lowest intake.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: These are the headline effect sizes regulators and plaintiffs' lawyers will cite; a 29 percent hypertension bump is large enough to drive reformulation pressure.
insights.locator: The analysis found that participants with the highest intake

insights.claim: Participants with the highest intake of antioxidant preservatives (including ascorbic acid, citric acid, erythorbates) showed a 22 percent higher risk of hypertension.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: That even 'antioxidant' preservatives long assumed benign carry risk widens the set of additives potentially exposed to reevaluation, including vitamin-C-derived ones.
insights.locator: Participants with the highest intake of antioxidant preservatives

insights.claim: Of the 17 most commonly consumed preservatives examined individually, eight were associated with increased hypertension risk: potassium sorbate (E202), potassium metabisulfite (E224), sodium nitrite (E250), ascorbic acid (E300), sodium ascorbate (E301), sodium erythorbate (E316), citric acid (E330), and rosemary extract (E392).
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: Names the specific E-numbers most at regulatory risk, letting investors map exposure to suppliers and product lines that rely on these additives.
insights.locator: The researchers also examined the 17 most commonly consumed preservatives

insights.claim: Among the eight flagged additives, ascorbic acid (E300) was the one also associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, not just hypertension.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: Singling out a compound marketed as vitamin C as carrying CVD risk is counterintuitive and could draw outsized attention and scrutiny.
insights.locator: Among these, ascorbic acid was also associated

insights.claim: During follow-up the study recorded 5,544 cases of hypertension and 2,450 cardiovascular-disease cases, including 1,142 cerebrovascular events and 1,308 cases of coronary artery disease.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: The large absolute event counts underpin the statistical power behind the associations, making them harder for industry to dismiss as noise.
insights.locator: During the follow-up period, researchers recorded 5,544 cases

insights.claim: About 16 percent of the association between non-antioxidant preservatives and cardiovascular disease was mediated indirectly through hypertension, suggesting preservatives may raise blood pressure which in turn raises CVD risk.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: A named mediation pathway strengthens the biological plausibility that regulators weigh, though the direct effect remains larger than the hypertension-mediated portion.
insights.quote: approximately 16 percent of the association between non-antioxidant preservatives and cardiovascular disease was mediated indirectly through hypertension
insights.locator: The study also found that approximately 16 percent

insights.claim: The researchers stress this is an observational study that does not establish a causal relationship, and note limitations including that women made up 78.7 percent of participants and the cohort skewed toward highly educated individuals.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: The observational caveat and skewed sample give industry a defense and cap how fast this can translate into binding rules, but the findings still shift the burden of proof.
insights.locator: The researchers emphasize that these findings come from an observational study

insights.claim: Despite the caveats, the statistical models adjusted for a wide range of confounders and the results held across multiple sensitivity analyses.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: Robustness across sensitivity analyses is what makes the signal durable enough to sustain regulatory momentum despite the observational limits.
insights.locator: the statistical models accounted for a wide range of potential confounding factors

insights.claim: The study's research director calls for the EFSA in Europe and the FDA in the USA to reevaluate the risks and benefits of these food additives for better consumer protection.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: An explicit call to EFSA and FDA is the clearest catalyst path; watch those agencies' dockets for reviews that could force reformulation across packaged foods.
insights.quote: These results suggest we need a reevaluation of the risks and benefits of these food additives by the authorities in charge, such as the EFSA in Europe and the FDA in the USA
insights.locator: Calls to Reevaluate Food Additive Regulations

insights.claim: Because these additives are consumed continuously across multiple foods without numerical limits on their use, the findings suggest it may be time to reopen the debate over whether existing regulations are adequate.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: The absence of cumulative-exposure limits is the specific regulatory gap most likely to be targeted, a potential structural change for how additives are permitted.
insights.locator: For additives that are consumed continuously through multiple foods

insights.claim: In the interim, researchers say the findings support existing recommendations to favor nonprocessed and minimally processed foods and to avoid unnecessary additives.
insights.speaker: Ritsuko Kawai
insights.so_what: Reinforces the secular consumer shift toward minimally processed foods, a demand tailwind for clean-label brands and headwind for additive-heavy packaged goods.
insights.quote: these findings support existing recommendations to favor nonprocessed and minimally processed foods, and avoid unnecessary additives
insights.locator: In the meantime, these findings support existing recommendations
